notes: Old-recipe chef review wave. Sources stay internal. Public recipe pages should read as confident cooking guidance.

recipes.review_summary: Boule is sound as a Chadian millet or sorghum flour staple cooked into a firm paste for sauces. The main improvement is presentation copy and clearer step wording.
recommended_changes: Keep the millet or sorghum base.
Keep salt optional and keep the method focused on lump-free stirring.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Regional cuisine references agree on millet or sorghum flour cooked with water into a firm staple.

sources.title: Sunheron - What to Eat in Chad

recipe.description: A smooth, firm millet or sorghum porridge shaped for scooping up Chadian sauces and stews.
recipe.story: Boule is the quiet anchor of many Chadian meals. Millet or sorghum flour is worked into boiling water until it turns smooth, firm, and elastic enough to tear by hand and scoop through stews.
recipe.regional_variations: Millet flour is common, but sorghum or maize flour may be used depending on the household and region.

replace_steps.title: Start the slurry
replace_steps.instruction: Bring most of the water to a boil in a heavy pot. In a bowl, mix 1 cup of millet flour with 1 cup cold water until smooth.
replace_steps.tip: A cold slurry helps stop lumps before they start.

replace_steps.title: Cook the base
replace_steps.instruction: Pour the slurry into the boiling water while stirring. Cook until it thickens and turns glossy.

replace_steps.title: Beat in the flour
replace_steps.instruction: Add the remaining flour gradually, stirring hard with a strong wooden spoon until the mixture becomes stiff.
replace_steps.tip: This is arm work. The smoother the stirring, the better the boule.

replace_steps.title: Finish the boule
replace_steps.instruction: Keep stirring over low heat until the boule pulls away from the pot and tastes fully cooked.

replace_steps.title: Shape and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Wet a bowl or spoon, shape the boule into rounds, and serve hot with stew or sauce.

recipes.review_summary: Daraba needed the full vegetable body made visible. Stronger Chadian-style references use okra, greens, tomato, peanut butter, and often sweet potato or eggplant with water or stock.
recommended_changes: Add water or stock, salt, pepper, sweet potato, and optional eggplant.
Cook firm vegetables before adding greens so the stew is creamy but not overcooked.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Chadian recipe references agree on okra, greens, peanut butter, tomatoes, and starchy vegetables.

sources.title: Tastes From The Road - Daraba from Chad

recipe.description: A Chadian peanut-okra stew with greens, tomato, sweet potato, and a creamy sauce made for boule or rice.
recipe.story: Daraba is the kind of stew that feeds a table without fuss. Okra gives it body, peanut butter gives it richness, and greens turn the pot earthy and generous.
recipe.regional_variations: Sweet potato, pumpkin, or eggplant can move in and out of the pot depending on the market.

replace_ingredients.name: mixed greens
replace_ingredients.prep_note: spinach, sorrel, cassava leaves, or sweet potato leaves, chopped

replace_steps.title: Start the base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a large pot. Cook onion until soft, then add tomatoes and scotch bonnet if using. Cook until the tomatoes collapse.

replace_steps.title: Simmer the firm vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add sweet potato, eggplant if using, okra, and water or stock. Simmer until the sweet potato is almost tender.

replace_steps.title: Stir in the peanut
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk peanut butter with a ladle of hot broth, then stir it back into the pot until the sauce turns creamy.
replace_steps.tip: Loosening the peanut butter first keeps the sauce smooth.

replace_steps.title: Add the greens
replace_steps.instruction: Fold in the chopped greens, cover, and cook until tender. The stew should be thick enough to sit on boule.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and black pepper. Serve hot with boule, rice, or sorghum.

recipes.review_summary: The millet porridge structure is sound. It needed salt and softer presentation language, with milk kept as a finishing choice rather than the whole cooking liquid.
recommended_changes: Add a pinch of salt.
Keep the porridge adjustable, from spoonable to drinkable.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Chadian references support la bouillie as millet, flour, rice, milk, sugar, lemon, or peanut-enriched breakfast porridge.

sources.title: Africa Inland Mission - Make Chad Porridge PDF
The Afrikan Store - Chad's Porridge Recipe

recipe.description: A warm Chadian millet porridge sweetened with sugar and finished with milk, vanilla, and lemon zest.
recipe.story: La Bouillie is morning comfort in a bowl. The millet slurry cooks into a smooth porridge, then milk, sugar, vanilla, and lemon make it gentle enough for breakfast or a light evening meal.

recipes.review_summary: The Comorian lobster and vanilla idea is right, but the sauce should be lighter and more elegant. Cream is common, while coconut cream is a reasonable island-style option.
recommended_changes: Reduce the cream volume.
Keep vanilla subtle and let the lobster stay central.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Strong culinary references identify langouste a la vanille as the Comoros national dish.

sources.title: Saveur - Grilled Lobsters With Vanilla Cream Sauce
International Cuisine - Comoros Lobster with Vanilla Sauce

recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice, sauteed greens, or pilao ya nazi
recipe.description: Comorian lobster finished in a delicate vanilla cream sauce, elegant without hiding the sweetness of the seafood.
recipe.story: Langouste a la Vanille works because the vanilla is gentle. It perfumes the cream, softens the wine and shallots, and lets the lobster taste like the sea.

replace_ingredients.name: lobster tails
replace_ingredients.substitution: small lobster, langoustines, shrimp, or scallops

replace_steps.title: Sear the lobster
replace_steps.instruction: Brush lobster with oil and sear flesh-side down until lightly golden. Remove before it is fully cooked.

replace_steps.title: Start the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Melt butter in the pan. Cook shallots until soft, then add white wine and reduce by half.

replace_steps.title: Infuse the vanilla
replace_steps.instruction: Add vanilla seeds, pods, and cream. Simmer gently until the sauce lightly coats a spoon.

replace_steps.title: Finish the lobster
replace_steps.instruction: Return lobster to the pan and cook gently until opaque and just firm.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Remove vanilla pods, season with salt and white pepper, and add a little lime if the sauce needs brightness.

recipes.review_summary: The old page treated Mkatra Foutra like a baked loaf. Stronger Comorian references describe it as a yeast coconut flatbread cooked on a pan and finished with sesame.
recommended_changes: Change the method from baked loaf to pan-cooked coconut flatbread.
Remove eggs, butter glaze, and loaf shaping from the public recipe.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Comoros-focused culinary references agree on coconut milk, yeast, flour, sesame, and skillet flatbread style.

sources.title: Eat This NY - Mkatra Foutra from Comoros

recipe.description: Comorian coconut flatbread made with yeast, coconut milk, and sesame, cooked on a hot pan until soft and golden.
recipe.story: Mkatra Foutra should feel like bread from the skillet, not cake from the oven. Coconut milk makes the dough tender, yeast gives it lift, and sesame seeds toast against the pan.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, honey, butter, fish curry, rougaille, or coconut-based stews
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks make smaller rounds, others cook one larger flatbread and cut it into wedges.

replace_steps.title: Mix the dough
replace_steps.instruction: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add warm coconut milk and knead until soft and smooth.

replace_steps.title: Let it rise
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and leave in a warm place until puffy and roughly doubled.

replace_steps.title: Shape the breads
replace_steps.instruction: Divide into 8 pieces. Flatten each into a round and press sesame seeds into one side.

replace_steps.title: Cook on the pan
replace_steps.instruction: Lightly oil a heavy pan and cook each round over medium heat until golden on both sides and cooked through.

replace_steps.title: Serve warm
replace_steps.instruction: Serve warm with tea, honey, butter, fish curry, or a coconut relish.

recipes.review_summary: Pilao ya Nazi is sound as a Comorian coconut rice pilaf. It mostly needed serving context and calmer copy.
recommended_changes: Keep coconut milk and warm spices.
Add clear serving pairings.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Comorian cuisine references support coconut-heavy rice and pilao as common island accompaniments.

recipe.best_served_with: Langouste a la Vanille, grilled fish, chicken curry, or tomato relishes
recipe.story: Pilao ya Nazi lets coconut do the quiet work. The rice steams with warm spices until the grains are fragrant, creamy at the edges, and ready to carry seafood or stew.
recipe.regional_variations: Some versions add meat or fish, while this one stays as a coconut rice side.

replace_steps.title: Bloom the spices
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until golden. Add cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and turmeric, then stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Toast the rice
replace_steps.instruction: Add rinsed rice and stir so every grain is coated in the spiced oil.

replace_steps.title: Steam
replace_steps.instruction: Add coconut milk, water, and salt. Bring to a boil, cover tightly, and cook on the lowest heat until the liquid is absorbed.

replace_steps.title: Rest
replace_steps.instruction: Take the pot off the heat and rest covered so the grains finish steaming.

replace_steps.title: Fluff and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Fluff gently, remove whole spices if desired, and serve with seafood, chicken, or vegetable stew.

recipes.review_summary: Liboke ya Mbika is directionally sound as pumpkin seed paste steamed in banana leaves. It needed salt and a small blending-liquid cue made visible.
recommended_changes: Add salt and a little water for grinding.
Keep banana leaf steaming as the core technique.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Congolese references support liboke as banana-leaf parcel cooking and mbika as pumpkin seed pudding.

recipe.story: Liboke is the art of letting banana leaves season the food while they protect it. Here, ground pumpkin seeds steam into a rich mbika parcel with tomato, onion, palm oil, and chile.
recipe.regional_variations: Fish, pork, cassava leaves, and pumpkin seed parcels all sit inside the wider liboke family.

recipes.review_summary: Moambe Chicken is sound as chicken in palm nut sauce, but the page omitted oil, salt, pepper, and an optional okra cue found in Congolese versions.
recommended_changes: Add oil, salt, pepper, and optional okra.
Keep palm nut cream as the heart of the sauce.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Multiple Congolese and Central African references identify chicken in palm nut sauce as a national or iconic dish.

recipe.description: Tender chicken simmered in palm nut sauce with tomato, onion, garlic, chile, and optional okra.
recipe.story: Moambe Chicken belongs to the Congo Basin table. The sauce should be deep orange, rich from palm nut cream, and thick enough to cling to chicken before it meets fufu, rice, or plantain.

replace_steps.title: Brown the chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and black pepper. Heat oil and brown the pieces on all sides, then remove them from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Cook the aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste and stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Build the sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, palm nut cream, water, and okra if using. Stir until smooth.

replace_steps.title: Simmer
replace_steps.instruction: Return chicken to the pot, add whole scotch bonnets, cover, and simmer until the chicken is tender and the sauce is thick.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole peppers, adjust seasoning, and serve with fufu, rice, or boiled plantains.

recipes.review_summary: Saka-Saka is right on cassava leaves, palm oil, fish, and long cooking. The page needed the common peanut paste option and more visible salt/water balance.
recommended_changes: Add peanut butter or ground peanuts.
Keep long cooking for cassava leaves.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Congolese sources consistently describe cassava leaves, palm oil, onion, fish or meat, and often peanut paste.

sources.title: Congolese cultural profile - Metro South Health PDF

recipe.story: Saka-Saka is a patient pot. Cassava leaves need time to soften, palm oil gives them depth, dried fish brings smoke, and peanut paste makes the greens round and satisfying.

replace_steps.title: Prepare the leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Pound or blend fresh cassava leaves very finely. If using frozen leaves, thaw and squeeze lightly.

replace_steps.title: Boil the greens
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer cassava leaves with water until they begin to soften.
replace_steps.tip: Cassava leaves are not a quick green. Give them time.

replace_steps.title: Build the flavor
replace_steps.instruction: In another pan, heat palm oil and cook onion until golden. Add garlic and fish, then stir into the cassava leaves.

replace_steps.title: Add peanut and slow-cook
replace_steps.instruction: Loosen peanut butter with hot cooking liquid and stir it into the pot. Add the whole chile if using and simmer until the leaves are tender and rich.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile, season with salt, and serve with fufu, boiled cassava, plantain, or rice.

recipes.review_summary: Laxoox is sound as a fermented one-sided flatbread. The fermentation timer was missing and the page needed serving context.
recommended_changes: Add the fermentation timer.
Keep one-sided cooking and bubble texture.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Horn of Africa references support laxoox/lahoh as sorghum-based fermented flatbread cooked on one side.

recipe.best_served_with: Honey and ghee, spiced tea, fah-fah, or tomato stews
recipe.story: Laxoox is breakfast bread with bubbles. The batter ferments until lively, then cooks on one side so the top stays spongy enough to catch honey, ghee, or stew.

replace_steps.title: Mix the batter
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk flours, yeast, sugar, salt, and warm water until smooth.

replace_steps.title: Ferment
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and let the batter stand until bubbly and lightly tangy.
replace_steps.tip: A warm kitchen gives better bubbles.

replace_steps.title: Cook one side
replace_steps.instruction: Pour a thin layer into a warm non-stick pan. Cook without flipping until the top is full of small holes and the edges lift.

replace_steps.title: Stack
replace_steps.instruction: Lift the laxoox carefully and stack on a plate. Repeat with the remaining batter.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve with honey and ghee for breakfast, or use it to scoop soup or stew.

recipes.review_summary: Fah-Fah is sound as a Djiboutian goat or meat soup with vegetables and warming spices. It needed serving context and cleaner step titles.
recommended_changes: Keep goat or lamb bones for broth depth.
Add laxoox or bread as the natural serving partner.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Djiboutian food references support goat or meat soup with vegetables as fah-fah.

recipe.best_served_with: Laxoox, crusty bread, rice, or lime wedges
recipe.story: Fah-Fah is broth with backbone. Bone-in goat gives the soup strength, potatoes and carrots make it a meal, and lime at the table wakes the whole bowl up.

replace_steps.title: Start the broth
replace_steps.instruction: Place goat pieces in a large pot with water. Bring to a boil and skim the foam.

replace_steps.title: Simmer the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and the whole chile. Simmer until the meat is tender.

replace_steps.title: Add vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add carrots and potatoes and keep simmering until the vegetables are tender.

replace_steps.title: Season
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile, season with salt, and check that the broth tastes full.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Ladle into bowls with meat and vegetables. Finish with cilantro and lime.

recipes.review_summary: Skoudehkaris was missing tomato paste, chile warmth, black pepper, and herbs that appear in stronger Djibouti references. The one-pot lamb and rice base is sound.
recommended_changes: Increase tomato presence.
Add tomato paste, chile, black pepper, and cilantro.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Djibouti-focused culinary references consistently show lamb or meat, rice, tomato, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, and chile.

recipe.best_served_with: Banana, salad, yogurt, or chile sauce
recipe.story: Skoudehkaris should smell like the port cities of the Horn: lamb, rice, tomato, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, and a little chile all settling into one pot.

replace_steps.title: Brown the lamb
replace_steps.instruction: Season lamb with salt and black pepper. Brown it in hot oil until the edges take color.

replace_steps.title: Cook the tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onions and cook until soft. Stir in garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, cardamom, cumin, cinnamon, turmeric, and chile.

replace_steps.title: Simmer the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock, cover, and simmer until the lamb is almost tender.

replace_steps.title: Steam the rice
replace_steps.instruction: Add drained rice to the pot. Cover tightly and cook on low until the rice absorbs the broth.

replace_steps.title: Rest and finish
replace_steps.instruction: Rest covered, fluff gently, scatter cilantro over the top, and serve with banana or salad.

recipes.review_summary: Ful Medames is sound, but the cook time was inconsistent with the dried-bean method and salt was missing from the visible ingredient list.
recommended_changes: Set cook time to match dried fava beans.
Add salt and keep olive oil, lemon, garlic, and cumin central.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Egypt-focused and museum references agree on fava beans, olive oil, cumin, lemon, and garlic.

sources.title: University of Michigan Kelsey Museum - Ful Medames PDF

recipe.story: Ful Medames is everyday Egyptian comfort. The beans should be creamy but not erased, bright with lemon, warm with cumin, and glossy with olive oil.

recipes.review_summary: Koshari was missing several ingredients used in the method: oil, cumin, salt, chile, and enough vinegar for both tomato sauce and dakka. Vermicelli is also a common rice component.
recommended_changes: Add oil, cumin, chile, salt, and enough vinegar.
Add vermicelli for the rice layer.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Egyptian references consistently describe rice, lentils, pasta, chickpeas, tomato sauce, garlic vinegar, and crispy onions.

sources.title: World Food Forum Recipe Booklet - Koshari

recipe.story: Koshari is Cairo in layers: rice, lentils, pasta, chickpeas, tomato sauce, garlicky vinegar, and a crown of onions fried until crisp.
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks fold vermicelli into the rice, some keep the rice plain. The tomato sauce, dakka, and fried onions carry the soul either way.

replace_steps.title: Cook the lentils
replace_steps.instruction: Boil lentils in salted water until tender but still holding shape. Drain and set aside.

replace_steps.title: Cook rice and pasta
replace_steps.instruction: Toast vermicelli in a little onion oil if using, then cook with rice. Boil macaroni separately until al dente.

replace_steps.title: Fry the onions
replace_steps.instruction: Fry sliced onions in batches until deep golden and crisp. Drain well and keep the onion oil.

replace_steps.title: Make tomato sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Cook half the garlic in a little onion oil. Add tomatoes, some vinegar, cumin, salt, and chile, then simmer until thick.

replace_steps.title: Make dakka
replace_steps.instruction: Sizzle the remaining garlic in oil, then add vinegar and cumin. It should smell sharp and garlicky.

replace_steps.title: Assemble
replace_steps.instruction: Layer rice, lentils, macaroni, and chickpeas. Spoon over tomato sauce and dakka, then finish with crispy onions.

recipes.review_summary: Molokhia is sound, but the broth aromatics used in the method were not visible in the ingredient list. The public copy also needed cleaner punctuation.
recommended_changes: Add onion, bay, cardamom, salt, and lemon wedges.
Keep the garlic-coriander tash-a as the defining finish.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Egyptian references agree on finely chopped jute leaves, chicken broth, garlic, coriander, and ghee or oil.

recipe.story: Molokhia is all about texture and aroma. The jute leaves make the soup silky, then hot ghee, garlic, and coriander hit the pot and announce dinner.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, vermicelli rice, roasted chicken, lemon, and bread

replace_steps.title: Make the broth
replace_steps.instruction: Poach chicken with onion, bay leaves, cardamom, salt, and pepper until cooked. Remove the chicken and strain the broth.

replace_steps.title: Cook the molokhia
replace_steps.instruction: Bring broth to a simmer, add chopped molokhia, and cook gently until the soup is silky. Do not boil hard.

replace_steps.title: Make the tash-a
replace_steps.instruction: Heat ghee, add garlic until golden, then stir in coriander for a few seconds until fragrant.
replace_steps.tip: Garlic moves from golden to burnt quickly, so stay close.

replace_steps.title: Finish the pot
replace_steps.instruction: Pour the sizzling garlic-coriander mixture into the molokhia and stir.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve with rice, chicken, lemon wedges, and bread.
